result:
I.  
The first thing to notice is the hum, a soft, almost sub-audible tremor that seems to come from the walls themselves. It is not the refrigerator, whose compressor cycles off and on with a predictable wheeze; nor is it the traffic on the boulevard three floors down, because at 3:12 a.m. even the ambularies have learned to coast. It is, I decide after three nights of insomnia, the building remembering. Concrete and rebar possess a patience we have forgotten. They store the frequencies of every argument, every laugh that ricocheted down the stairwell, every hand that dragged along the banister. When the human racket subsides, the edifice rehearses its archive, the way an old man hums lullabies to himself in the dark.

I discovered this while trying to write a letter I will never send. On the screen: “Dear R.,” and then nothing for thirty-six lines. The cursor blinks like a metronome keeping time for nobody. I used to believe writer’s block was a failure of character; now I think it is merely courtesy. The sentences are waiting for the walls to finish their recital. If I try to force them, they arrive brittle, offended, like children dragged from a bedtime story mid-cliffhanger.

II.  
When I was eight I owned a kaleidoscope made of plastic light enough to float. Inside, the chips were not the usual confetti of colored paper but tiny shells, snail shells scavenged from a beach in Normandy, bleached white by salt and neglect. I believed—persisted in believing—that if I twisted the barrel slowly enough the shells would align into a map that would tell me where I had lost something I could not name. I spent whole summers chasing that map. My mother, baffled but solicitous, would bring me lemonade and leave without speaking. Years later I understood the thing I was missing was the future, and the future, like salt on shells, works by erosion, not assembly.

III.  
There is a kind of time that lives inside unfinished tasks. A half-knitted scarf in a basket under the couch stores an interval you can revisit, exactly the same, each time you lift the lid. My basket holds a scarf I started fifteen years ago, cornflower blue wool meant for someone I no longer love. Yet when I touch the stitches I am instantly eight-and-twenty again, walking across the Grünerløkka bridge in Oslo, February dusk, the air tasting of sea and diesel. The scarf will never be finished; this is its gift. It keeps the hour suspended, a snow globe without snow, liquid sloshing softly when the bus lumbers past.

IV.  
I talk to the radio in the car the way sailors talk to the horizon. The radio seldom answers, but last week, on a stretch of I-5 south of Bakersfield, the station cut from a stale hit to a voice that said, “You were right to leave the gas burner on low.” Just that. Then back to the chorus. I checked the rear-view mirror: nothing but almond orchards receding. Still, I turned the knob to see if the sentence had a sequel; it didn’t. That evening I checked every burner twice before sleeping, not from fear but homage. Somewhere, a stranger had entrusted me with a fragment of cautionary telepathy; refusal would be arrogance.

V.  
My father kept a notebook in which he recorded only numbers: 47, 1230, 88, 9. No units, no dates. After he died I thumbed through looking for a cipher and found only the implacable austerity of quantity. I made histograms, tried Fibonacci, tried Morse. Nothing. Finally, I took the notebook to the Pacific at Half Moon Bay and let the tide have it. The pages dissolved like communion wafers, releasing columns of mute integers into the foam. The ocean did not explain them, but it accepted them without irritation, the way an old dog accepts a stranger’s hand on its head. I walked home lighter, though I had learned nothing. Sometimes surrender is the only hermeneutics we need.

VI.  
Cities, too, keep notebooks, but theirs are built of neon and pigeon footprints. Cross a bridge at 4 p.m. and again at 4 a.m.; you will traverse the same geometry, yet the city has rewritten its marginalia. The graffiti that read “We are the revolution” has been replaced by “Please don’t feed the rats,” and both have merit. Revolutions evolve; rats persist. I once tried photographing the same alley every day for a year. On day 127 a man stepped into the frame and asked if I was documenting or stealing. I told him the truth: I don’t know. He laughed so hard he had to sit on a fire hydrant. Then he asked me to take his portrait. I did. He insisted on holding a newspaper from 1989 announcing the fall of the Berlin Wall. The next day he was gone; the newspaper stayed, flapping like a wounded gull until the rain liquefied it. I still have the photo. His eyes say: history is a rumor we choose to repeat.

VII.  
The problem with memory is it improves the past the way Photoshop improves skin: by deleting blemishes we needed for recognition. I can no longer recall the exact sourness of kindergarten paint; I remember only the idea of sourness, which is useless. Yet if I buy a jar of tempera and stick my tongue into it (don’t), the original shock returns intact, like a cat that pretended to run away. This suggests that experience is stored elsewhere, perhaps in the tongue, perhaps in the palm of the hand that held the brush. The brain merely keeps the index. When people lose their hippocampus they can still cry at melodies they cannot name. Retrieval is a courtesy, not a statute.

VIII.  
I have lived in nine apartments and loved ten people. One of them loved me back with mathematics: she calculated the exact wattage needed to keep a basil plant alive on a windowsill at 41 degrees north latitude, then applied the formula to the relationship. We lasted four seasons, like the plant. When she moved out she left a note: “The constants were stronger than the variables.” I kept the plant another year out of gratitude. It flowered, small white stars I had not earned. I gave the leaves to strangers on the bus, telling them it brought luck. One woman wrote me later to say she used them in soup and her insomnia vanished. She asked the plant’s name. I lied and said it was called “Second Chances.” The plant died anyway, but the woman and I still send postcards. Hers always arrive with a pressed leaf taped inside, unsigned. I keep them in a shoebox labeled “Unread Mail,” even though I read them every solstice.

IX.  
Freedom is sometimes defined as the space between two prohibitions. In that gap a dog can bark once, a child can drop an ice cream, and a war can begin. I walked through such a gap in Sarajevo in 2019, a narrow alley where the stones are pocked like moon craters. A café had placed tiny round tables in the crater-shadows; people drank espresso amid the historical stigmata. No one spoke above a whisper. The owner told me the most popular item was the “uncertain futures” cocktail: plum rakija, sugar cube, twist of orange, served flaming. You must drink it before the flame dies or your wish will invert. I asked what happened if you didn’t have a wish. He said, “Then the drink makes one for you, and they are never kind.” I drank too slowly; the flame guttered. On the walk back to the hostel I invented a desire so fierce it frightened me: to remember the moment I forgot my mother’s face. It came true before morning.

X.  
The etymology of the word “nostalgia” contains the Greek algos, pain. We think of homesickness as soft, but it is a drilling ache that hollows the sternum. A more accurate rendition would be “home-ache.” I carry mine in the left pocket of every coat, shaped like a subway token from a city that no longer uses tokens. The machines now scan phones; the turnstiles glow with algorithmic mercy. Still, I palm the coin when passing through, a clandestine sacrament. Sometimes I press it into a stranger’s hand and keep walking. One day I doubled back and saw the stranger standing exactly where I left him, staring at the token as if it contained coordinates. Maybe it does. Maybe someday he’ll mail it to me with no return address and the cycle will close like a bracelet.

XI.  
The moment I realized my grandmother had become a child again was when she asked me to peel an orange and refused to eat it because “the segments look too much like lungs.” She had been a nurse for forty years; anatomy once carried no metaphors. I sat beside her hospital bed and recited ingredients of the shampoo she used when I was small: camomile, nettle, rosemary. She repeated the words like sea shells finding their way back to surf. When I left, she pressed an orange pip into my palm and closed my fist so tightly the fingernails left crescents in the skin. I planted it in a terracotta pot on the fire escape. It never sprouted, but every January I keep the soil damp out of respect for unintended orchards.

XII.  
The trouble with mirrors is they age in reverse. Each morning I confront a face slightly younger than the one I wore to sleep, as if dreams refund epidermal increments. My left eye carries a floater shaped like a hummingbird; I have named it Lucille after a woman who once told me the shortest measurable unit of time is the instant between a stoplight turning green and the driver behind you honking. Lucille hovers in the vitreous, reminding me that perception itself has floaters, blind spots we learn to work around. I spoke to an ophthalmologist about it; he said the brain is very good at subtraction. If it cannot delete the speck, it deletes the memory of the speck. I asked whether the same applied to guilt. He said guilt is vascular, not optical. I left with new vocabulary and the same hummingbird.

XIII.  
There is a species of squid that edits its own RNA on the fly, recoding neurons to match changes in depth, temperature, threat. Humans call it “extraordinary.” The squid, presumably, does not call it anything; it simply becomes what the moment demands. I keep a photo of one taped above the desk where I pay bills. The tentacles are spread like spilled knitting needles, the eye a wet coin of curiosity. I look at it when the sentence refuses to turn. The message is not poetic; it is instructional. Edit yourself, but do it faster than regret. I tore it down once after a breakup; the empty rectangle on the wall looked more judgmental than the cephalopod, so I put it back.

XIV.  
The longest walk I ever took was across the airport in Minneapolis after learning my flight had been cancelled due to un-manifested fog. The terminal stretched like a salt flat. I carried nothing but a paperback of Neruda and a bagel that shed poppy seeds like black confetti. Halfway, I began to count my steps in sets of fourteen, the number of lines in a sonnet. By the time I reached the customer-service desk I had composed, without writing, a crown of sonnets in which every terminal became a stanza and every delay an enjambment. The woman rebooking tickets asked why I was smiling. I told her I had just crossed an entire continent of iambs. She handed me a voucher for dinner: “Poets pay in abstractions, but vouchers spend like money.” I ate the worst pad thai of my life and still remember it fondly.

XV.  
We speak of “finding” love, yet nobody describes the moment of “losing” it as a find. Still, loss can be excavation. When my cat died—I use the euphemism “crossed the rainbow bridge,” but she would have preferred the blunt truth—I dug a hole in the backyard and struck clay so hard the shovel rang like a bell. I wrapped her in the sweater she loved to knead, grey cashmere pocked with pulled threads. As I covered her, the soil clattered like coins. I had the irrational thought that I was burying not a body but a collection of nights: the weight of her on my feet, the 3 a.m. yowl at the wall where she saw things I couldn’t. I half-expected the hole to glow, but it behaved like ordinary ground. A month later, lavender sprouted at the spot. I tell myself it’s happenstance. I also tell myself that if I ever need proof of persistence I can dig and find the bones arranged into a question mark. I never will; the possibility suffices.

XVI.  
Entropy is not disorder but the revelation of how many arrangements are possible. A broken coffee cup on the kitchen floor is merely exploring states the factory discouraged. I swept it up once and tried to reassemble the pieces with superglue. The result looked like a map of lost continents. I placed it on the windowsill and used it as a planter for succulents. They thrived in the fissures, their roots following cracks the way water follows prayer. When guests asked I called it the “ex-cup,” and if they misheard as “excuse” I did not correct them. We all need excuses to grow.

XVII.  
In the age of streaming, the word “buffer” has become theological. We watch a circle spin and are reminded that time is granular, not fluid. I have begun to treat buffering as an oracle: whatever thought surfaces during the pause is the one I was avoiding. Last week, waiting for a film to resume, I remembered I had forgotten to call my cousin on the anniversary of her mastectomy. I texted immediately; she replied with a voice memo in which ambient hospital sounds chimed in the background. She was there for a check-up, everything fine. She said my text arrived exactly as the doctor said, “See you next year.” She took it as a sign. I took it as buffering.

XVIII.  
I have never learned to whistle with two fingers, and this feels like a character flaw. The people who can emit those piercing chords seem to possess authority over crowds, dogs, and fate. I practiced in the shower until I hyperventilated. Nothing. My aunt could whistle Bach’s Badinerie and make birds fall silent, straining to understand. She claimed the secret was not the fingers but the gap between them, a resonating chamber shaped like hope. After she died I tried again, fingers still awkward. Instead, I exhaled a note so soft only the dog cocked his head. It was a beginning, though of what I don’t yet know.

XIX.  
The difference between exile and travel is luggage. Travelers pack contingency; exiles pack memory. I met a man in Lisbon who carried only a tin box that once held sardines. Inside: a rusted key, a button shaped like an acorn, and a photograph of a house with the roof burned off. He invited me for coffee and showed each item as if translating sacred texts. The key belonged to a door that no longer existed; the acorn button had fallen off the coat of a woman who became a continent he would never revisit; the photograph was taken by him at age twelve, the morning after the fire. He said exile began not when he left but when the house grew back its roof without him. I asked if he planned to return. He smiled, tapped the tin. “I already carry the blueprint.” We parted at the tram stop. I watched the tram erase him section by section, like a sentence deleted in slow motion.

XX.  
The last page of a notebook always feels like a cliff. I used to leave it blank to appease the gods of continuity. Then I read that in some tribes the youngest member must speak the final words of a burial, ensuring the story keeps growing even underground. Now I fill that page with a single line from a stranger: “The ocean is only lonely between boats.” I write it, close the book, and begin another. The statement may be true, may be nonsense, but it travels forward like ballast, preventing the whole vessel from tipping into certainty.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
